_readme: REAL DATA, finally. Source: 'Collection of Farm Activities Data and Other Related Studies', Directorate of Economics & Statistics, Government of Maharashtra, Volume I (state-level results), published 29 June 2021, field survey reference year 2018-19, covering 34 districts (Pune among them) via CAPI interviews with 13,600 farmers. Cost concepts: Cost A = paid-out costs + depreciation + land revenue; Cost B = Cost A + rental value of land + interest on fixed capital; Cost C = Cost B + imputed family labour (the figure closest to true economic cost). Uploaded by Bade as Collection_Volume_I_Final.pdf.
critical_caveats: These are STATE-LEVEL averages (all 34 surveyed districts pooled), not Pune-district-specific, EXCEPT where explicitly marked pune_specific below — those come from the report's district-range callouts, where Pune happened to be the cited min/max district.
District-level breakdowns for every crop exist in Volume II of this same report, which we do not yet have. That's the next real gap to close.

Data is dated 2018-19 (reference year) — roughly 7-8 years old as of July 2026. Input costs (seed, fertilizer, labour) have almost certainly risen since then; using these Cost C figures unadjusted will UNDERSTATE current cultivation cost. Needs an inflation/escalation factor before real use — not yet applied here.
1 hectare = 2.471 acres; acre figures below are direct conversions from the report's per-hectare figures.

Onion.note: Negative return in the survey year at Cost C — report explicitly flags Onion as one of the few crops NOT giving good overall returns in 2018-19.

Tomato.pune_specific: Pune recorded the LOWEST tomato price realization of any surveyed district (₹638/qtl vs state avg ₹1,155.84/qtl) — worth flagging to a Pune tomato farmer directly.

Wheat.pune_specific: Pune recorded the LOWEST wheat working capital (cost) of any surveyed district (₹17,118.22/ha vs ₹38,513.56/ha in Akola) — suggests genuinely favorable wheat-growing cost conditions in Pune district.

Gram_Chana.note: Report's incomplete extraction — max productivity and max price district not fully captured from this pass, need to revisit source PDF page for Gram.

Tur_PigeonPea.note: Report notes Tur was the only crop in the entire survey where intercropping was reported among sampled farmers — relevant since we already model Tur as a rotation-friendly legume.

Turmeric.note: High absolute cost per hectare reflects long duration (~9 months) and heavy input needs — matches our engine's earlier finding that Turmeric is high-mean, high-absolute-variance.

Rice_Bhat.note: Kharif Paddy. Max productivity/price district not fully captured this pass.

Jowar_Sorghum.note: Jowar grown both Kharif and Rabi in Maharashtra — report gives separate figures for each. Report explicitly flags Jowar as one of the few crops NOT giving good overall returns.

Groundnut.note: Also grown in summer season per the survey (Cost C ₹81,340.44/ha, ₹3,135.89/qtl) — very similar to kharif figures.

productivity_range_qtl_per_ha.state_avg_note: 189.00 qtl/ha cited as one end of range

Grapes.note: Perennial — excluded from our rotation engine by design, but this is real baseline data for the 'multi-year land commitment' comparison we said we'd show separately.

Pomegranate.pune_specific: Pune is explicitly cited as the productivity anchor point (101.21 qtl/ha) in the district range — Purandar taluka's pomegranate specialty shows up directly in this national dataset.

Fig_Anjeer.pune_specific: Fig had only 1 district in the entire survey sample — and it was Pune (Purandar taluka's fig specialty). Sample size is very small (20 farmers) so precision is lower than other crops, but this is literally Pune-only data.
Fig_Anjeer.note: Cost A/B/C figures not captured in this extraction pass — need to revisit source PDF for the Fig cost table specifically.

contract_farming_evidence_from_same_repo._note: Directly relevant to the selling-channel comparison feature — real Maharashtra evidence that contract farming can beat open-market mandi sale, from this same DES study.
contract_farming_evidence_from_same_repo.white_onion: Cost of production ₹387.32/qtl under contract farming; contract price ₹500.00/qtl; net return ₹112.68/qtl — while open-market onion growers suffered losses in the same survey year (matches the negative Cost-C return we recorded above for regular onion).
contract_farming_evidence_from_same_repo.potato: Cost of production ₹933/qtl under contract farming; contract price ₹1,700/qtl; surplus ₹767/qtl — explicitly described in the report as 'much higher than cultivating potato to be sold in open market.'
